message.content:
Fix BLOCKER/MAJOR issues in /Users/gbang/Downloads/algora_java/workspace/java-lld-parking-001_lld/.

Issues:
1. [MAJOR] _response.md §9 — three locations: the 'Deadlock' row of the concurrency-vocabulary table ('avoided: we hold only ONE spot lock at a time'); the 'Why ReentrantLock' Q&A ('Today my call graph holds one spot lock at a time'); and the 'What's the deadlock risk?' Q&A ('a park holds exactly ONE spot lock and never grabs a second while holding it'). The code is ParkingLotService.park() lines 107-119.: The document's central deadlock-safety argument is factually FALSE and self-contradictory. park() does `return withSpotGuard(candidate, () -> { if (!candidate.isFree()) return park(vehicle); ... })` — i.e. when the re-check fails it calls park() RECURSIVELY while still holding candidate's lock, and the recursive call acquires a DIFFERENT spot's lock. I proved this empirically with an instrumented subclass under a 128-thread / 30k-iteration churn: a single thread holds up to 4 spot locks simultaneously (MAX simultaneous spot-locks held = 4). So the claims 'one spot lock at a time' and 'never grabs a second while holding it' are wrong. The doc even acknowledges the recursion in §5.5 ('the loser's re-check at step 5 sees OCCUPIED and recurses to L1-2') but never reconciles that with the deadlock table — the two statements directly contradict each other in the same document. An interviewer grading the per-spot-lock angle would immediately catch this: 'You told me you never hold two locks, but your retry recurses while holding the first lock.' Note also: the code does NOT actually deadlock (a held spot is always OCCUPIED, so the strategy never re-offers it → no AB-BA lock-ordering cycle can form; 200 stampede trials and the churn stress all passed clean), so this is a documentation/reasoning defect, not a live bug — hence MAJOR not BLOCKER.
   Fix: Either (a) fix the narrative to tell the truth: 'the retry recurses while still holding the candidate's lock, so a thread can transiently hold several spot locks; this is still deadlock-free because a held spot is OCCUPIED and the strategy never re-offers an occupied spot, so no lock-ordering cycle can form' — and drop the false 'only ONE lock' / 'never grabs a second' lines; OR (b) change the code to not recurse under the lock: release the candidate's lock before retrying (e.g. return a sentinel from withSpotGuard and loop in park() OUTSIDE the guard), which makes the 'one lock at a time' claim actually true and also avoids holding the loser's lock during the entire retry.

Fix CODE (not test unless test itself is weak). Keep Java 17+ idioms, one base exception, no ... bodies. After fix, run mvn test, confirm green. Update §7 in _response.md if output changed. Return what changed + final test status.

1	# Design a Parking Lot (Java 17+, thread-safe, in-memory)
2	
3	> Mode for this writeup: **Mode B — Coding environment (Uber/Meta style).** I write the code and RUN it. §7 shows real `mvn` output.
4	
5	## ⏱️ 60-Minute Interview Plan
6	
7	| Time | What you do | Mode |
8	|------|-------------|------|
9	| 0–5 min | **Say §1-2 out loud** — restate "in-memory thread-safe parking lot", propose scope cuts. Don't write yet. | 🗣️ SAY |
10	| 5–8 min | **Pin the API surface (§3.5)** — `park`, `unpark`, plus two read methods. "Here's the contract before I design classes." | 🗣️ SAY |
11	| 8–14 min | **Draw the core class diagram** — 5 boxes: `ParkingLotService`, `ParkingSpot`, `SpotAllocationStrategy`, `BestFitNearestStrategy`, `ThreadSafeParkingLotService`. Mention `Vehicle`/`Ticket`/`ParkingFloor` verbally. | ✍️ DRAW |
12	| 14–16 min | **State patterns + concurrency flag** — Strategy + Template Method, then: "I see the double-allocation race in `park` — two cars see the same spot free. I'll fix it with per-spot locks at the end." | 🗣️ SAY |
13	| 16–18 min | **Walk §5.5 approach** — key insight: spot is a self-guarding state machine + each spot carries its own lock; selection is a pluggable strategy. | 🗣️ SAY |
14	| 18–38 min | **Write code in interview order** — enums → `ParkingSpot` state machine + per-spot lock → `SpotAllocationStrategy` + best-fit → `ParkingLotService` skeleton → `park()` → `unpark()`. | ✍️ WRITE |
15	| 38–45 min | **Demo Case 1 + Case 3** — run `Main`, point at output. | 🗣️ SAY / run |
16	| 45–55 min | **Concurrency answer** — per-spot `ReentrantLock`, no-op `withSpotGuard` hook, thread-safe subclass. Write the subclass only if asked. | 🗣️ SAY (❓ code if asked) |
17	| 55–60 min | **Follow-ups** — RLock vs Lock, per-spot vs global, distributed CAS. | ❓ IF ASKED |
18	
19	**Two interview modes:**
20	- **Mode A — Whiteboard / no-run (Amazon, Google):** Skip §7 run output, demo verbally.
21	- **Mode B — Coding environment (Uber, Meta):** Run `Main` in §7. This writeup is Mode B.
22	
23	**The one move that flags "strong hire":** right after the diagram, say "I see a double-allocation race in `park`" — before being asked.
24	
25	---
26	
27	## 1. Understanding the Problem & Clarifying Questions
28	
29	### 1.1 What we're building (plain words)
30	
31	> 💬 "We're building the brain of a parking garage that lives in memory. Cars, motorcycles, and handicapped vehicles drive up; the system finds them the best free spot across multiple floors, hands back a ticket, and when they leave it frees the spot and charges them for how long they stayed. The two hard parts are picking the *right* spot — smallest one that fits, nearest floor — and making sure two cars arriving at the exact same second never get handed the same spot."
32	
33	### 1.2 Clarifying questions (candidate-led proposals)
34	
35	**You:** "For v1 I'll assume a single in-memory lot in one process — no database, no multi-region. The whole thing is one JVM. Sound right?"
36	**Interviewer:** "Yep, in-memory is the scope."
37	*✓ scoped: one process, in-memory state — distributed is a §9 'if we scaled' note.*
38	
39	**You:** "Spot sizes: I'll model compact, large, and handicapped. A motorcycle fits a compact or larger, a car needs at least a large, and a handicapped vehicle needs a handicapped spot — and a regular car must NOT be allowed to take a handicapped spot. OK?"
40	**Interviewer:** "Agreed — and yes, protect the handicapped spots."
41	*✓ locked: handicapped is a reserved category matched by exact equality, not by size ranking.*
42	
43	**You:** "Allocation policy: I'll do best-fit — smallest spot that still fits — breaking ties by nearest floor. I'll make that a pluggable strategy so we can swap to 'cheapest first' or 'random' later without touching core code. Reasonable?"
44	**Interviewer:** "Love it, make it pluggable."
45	*✓ Strategy seam is in scope — this is the design's smart move.*
46	
47	**You:** "Billing: charge by time, per started hour, rounding partial hours up, with a one-hour minimum. I'll keep money in cents to avoid float bugs. Fine?"
48	**Interviewer:** "Sounds good. Flat hourly rate is fine for now."
49	*✓ scoped: flat rate, round up, cents. Tiered/peak pricing is a future strategy.*
50	
51	**You:** "Concurrency is the meat: many cars park and unpark at once. I'll keep the core single-threaded-clean and then show a thread-safe version using a per-spot lock so two cars never double-book one spot. I'll prove it with a 50-thread race test. That's the deliverable you care about?"
52	**Interviewer:** "Exactly — show me no double-allocation under contention."
53	*✓ the concurrency angle is per-spot fine-grained locks; the race test is the proof.*
54	
55	**You:** "I'll treat the physical gate hardware, license-plate OCR, and payment processing as out of scope — we just return a fee amount. OK?"
56	**Interviewer:** "Agreed, out of scope."
57	*✓ system boundary: we compute the fee, we don't move money or open gates.*
58	
59	### 1.3 Final Requirements (the whiteboard summary)
60	
61	**Functional Requirements**
62	
63	1. **A vehicle parks** by driving up with a known type (motorcycle / car / handicapped).
64	   - System finds the best free *compatible* spot across all floors and marks it occupied
65	   - Returns a `Ticket` (spot id, vehicle, entry time) on success
66	   - Throws `NoSpotAvailableException` if no compatible spot is free
67	2. **A vehicle unparks** by presenting its ticket id.
68	   - System frees the spot and returns the fee (cents)
69	   - Throws `TicketNotFoundException` for an unknown or already-used ticket
70	3. **Allocation is best-fit, nearest-floor** and pluggable.
71	   - Smallest fitting spot wins; ties broken by lowest floor, then spot id
72	   - The policy is a Strategy — swappable without core changes
73	4. **Billing by time** — per started hour, rounded up, one-hour minimum, in cents.
74	5. **Concurrent park/unpark are safe** — no two vehicles ever get the same spot.
75	   - N threads parking into K spots → exactly K succeed on K *distinct* spots; the rest get the typed full error
76	6. **Read queries** — `occupiedCount()`, `availableCountFor(type)`.
77	
78	**Out of scope**
79	- Physical gate / barrier hardware (downstream — we just say "occupied")
80	- Payment processing (we return a fee number; money movement is a separate system)
81	- License-plate recognition (input is a given `Vehicle`)
82	- Multi-process / multi-region (in-memory single JVM for v1)
83	
84	> 💬 **What you say to open:** "OK, before I model anything, the one thing I want to lock down is the concurrency contract — N cars racing for K spots must yield exactly K winners on K distinct spots. With that pinned, let me walk through the actors and flows."
85	
86	---
87	
88	## 2. Actors & Core Flows
89	
90	**Actors**
91	- **Driver / vehicle** — drives up to park, presents a ticket to leave. Initiates both core flows.
92	- **Lot operator (config)** — builds the lot (floors and spots) at startup. Not a runtime actor.
93	- **Billing reader** — whoever reads the fee returned by `unpark` (a kiosk / payment system downstream).
94	
95	**Core flows**
96	1. Park: driver arrives → strategy picks best free compatible spot → spot marked occupied → ticket returned
97	2. Unpark: driver presents ticket → system finds spot → spot freed → fee computed and returned
98	3. Reject: driver arrives, no compatible spot free → typed `NoSpotAvailableException` (never a silent null)
99	
100	---
101	
102	## 3. Core Entities (with explicit accept / reject reasoning)
103	
104	### 3.1 Candidate noun walkthrough
105	
106	> **ParkingLot / Lot** — The whole system. It owns the floors, the open-ticket registry, the clock and rate, and coordinates park/unpark. This is the orchestrator. *Accepted — it becomes `ParkingLotService`.*
107	>
108	> **ParkingFloor** — A real grouping of spots with a floor number that drives "nearest". It owns its spots (composition). *Accepted.*
109	>
110	> **ParkingSpot** — A physical spot with an id, a size, and an occupancy state — AND it carries its own lock. It has data plus behavior (occupy/release as a guarded state machine). This is the heart of the design. *Accepted.*
111	>
112	> **Vehicle** — What's parking. We care about its plate (identity on the ticket) and its type (drives compatibility). It never changes after creation. *Accepted — as an immutable record.*
113	>
114	> **Ticket** — The entry receipt: spot id, vehicle, entry timestamp. It has its own identity and an immutable lifecycle. Worth its own type so unpark can look it up and billing can read the entry time. *Accepted — immutable record.*
115	>
116	> **AllocationStrategy** — "How do we pick a spot?" is a policy that will change. Pulling it behind an interface is the smart move here. *Accepted — Strategy interface.*
117	>
118	> **Fee / Price** — A number computed at exit from entry time and rate. It has no lifecycle of its own. *Rejected — it's a `long` (cents) returned by `unpark`, computed by a private helper.*
119	>
120	> **Size / VehicleType / Status** — Finite sets of values with a compatibility rule. *Rejected as entities — modeled as enums (`SpotSize`, `VehicleType`, `SpotStatus`). The "fits" rule lives on `VehicleType`.*
121	>
122	> **Driver / Customer** — Actors who call the system; we don't store them. *Rejected — actors live outside the model.*
123	>
124	> **Gate / Barrier / Payment** — Out of system scope per §1.3. *Rejected — external systems.*
125	
126	### 3.2 Final entity table
127	
128	| Entity | Responsibility | Key fields (typed) | Invariant it guards |
129	|---|---|---|---|
130	| `ParkingLotService` | Coordinate park/unpark; own the truth about who's parked | `floors: List<ParkingFloor>`, `strategy: SpotAllocationStrategy`, `clock: Supplier<Instant>`, `ratePerHourCents: long`, `openTickets: Map<String,Ticket>` (key = spot id), `ticketCounter: AtomicLong` | Every parked car has exactly one open ticket; a freed spot is removed from the registry |
131	| `ParkingFloor` | Own a labelled bag of spots on one floor | `floorNumber: int`, `spots: List<ParkingSpot>` | Owns its spots; outsiders get an unmodifiable view |
132	| `ParkingSpot` | Hold one car; guard its own status; carry its own lock | `id: String`, `floorNumber: int`, `size: SpotSize`, `status: SpotStatus`, `vehicle: @Nullable Vehicle`, `lock: ReentrantLock` | A FREE spot can be occupied once; only OCCUPIED→FREE on release; release is a no-op otherwise |
133	| `Vehicle` | Identify what's parking and its type | `licensePlate: String`, `type: VehicleType` | Non-blank plate, non-null type (validated at construction) |
134	| `Ticket` | The entry receipt for billing & unpark | `id: String`, `spotId: String`, `vehicle: Vehicle`, `entryTime: Instant` | Immutable; all fields non-null |
135	| `SpotAllocationStrategy` | Decide which free spot to use (policy) | *(interface)* `findSpot(floors, type): Optional<ParkingSpot>` | Returns a *candidate* only; never mutates state |
136	
137	**Enums** — `SpotSize {COMPACT, LARGE, HANDICAPPED}` (each carries a `rank`); `VehicleType {MOTORCYCLE, CAR, HANDICAPPED}` (each owns a `fits(spotSize)` rule); `SpotStatus {FREE, OCCUPIED}`.
138	
139	### 3.3 🎙️ Spoken intro
140	
141	> 💬 "So the model has six objects. `ParkingLotService` is the brain — it owns the floors and a map of open tickets, keyed by spot id so unpark is O(1) on the spot. `ParkingSpot` is the interesting one: it remembers its own physical state and carries its own lock — it's the only thing allowed to flip itself occupied or free, and to take a spot you lock that one spot. `SpotAllocationStrategy` is the seam — the lot asks it which spot, so the policy is swappable. `Vehicle` and `Ticket` are immutable records — plain data. The pieces I deliberately left OUT are Fee, which is just a number computed at exit, and Driver/Gate/Payment, which live outside our boundary."
142	
143	---
144	
145	## 3.5 API / System Interface
146	
147	| Method | Signature | Returns | Throws | Purpose |
148	|---|---|---|---|---|
149	| `park` | `park(Vehicle vehicle)` | `Ticket` — the entry receipt | `ParkingException` (null vehicle), `NoSpotAvailableException` | A driver arrives; system picks the best free compatible spot and issues a ticket |
150	| `unpark` | `unpark(String ticketId)` | `long` — fee in cents | `TicketNotFoundException` | A driver leaves; system frees the spot and bills by time |
151	| `occupiedCount` | `occupiedCount()` | `int` — number of parked cars | — | How full is the lot right now (O(1) registry size) |
152	| `availableCountFor` | `availableCountFor(VehicleType type)` | `long` — free compatible spots | — | How many spots a given vehicle type could still use |
153	
154	> 🎙️ "The whole system is four public calls — two that change state (`park`, `unpark`) and two read-only queries. Everything else is internal: the spot guards itself, the strategy picks the spot, billing is a private helper. Handed this interface cold, you'd know exactly what the system does without seeing a class."
155	
156	---
157	
158	## 4. Class Design
159	
160	### 4.1 Per-class derivation
161	
162	#### (1) `ParkingLotService` — the orchestrator
163	
164	**(a) Role** — The system's public API. External code calls it to park and unpark; it coordinates the strategy and the spots and owns the open-ticket registry.
165	
166	**(b) State derivation**
167	
168	| Requirement | What this class must remember |
169	|---|---|
170	| "find the best free compatible spot across all floors" | The list of floors |
171	| "allocation is pluggable" | A reference to the allocation strategy |
172	| "bill by time" | The clock (injected) and the hourly rate |
173	| "unpark by ticket; never strand a car" | A registry of open tickets, keyed by spot id |
174	| "readable ticket ids" | A ticket counter |
175	
176	```
177	class ParkingLotService:
178	    - floors: List<ParkingFloor>
179	    - strategy: SpotAllocationStrategy
180	    - clock: Supplier<Instant>
181	    - ratePerHourCents: long
182	    - openTickets: Map<String, Ticket>   # key = spot id
183	    - ticketCounter: AtomicLong
184	```
185	
186	**State placement trade-off** — Occupancy could live on the orchestrator (a set of taken spot ids) OR on the spot itself. I put the *physical* status (FREE/OCCUPIED + which vehicle) on the `ParkingSpot` because that describes the spot's own condition, and I put the *relational* fact (which ticket maps to which spot) in the orchestrator's registry because that's a system-managed relationship. Physical state on the entity, relational state in the orchestrator.
187	
188	**(c) Operations derivation**
189	
190	| Need from requirements | Method on this class |
191	|---|---|
192	| "a vehicle parks → ticket or full error" | `park(vehicle) -> Ticket` |
193	| "a vehicle unparks → fee" | `unpark(ticketId) -> long` |
194	| "how full is the lot" | `occupiedCount() -> int` |
195	| "how many spots free for a type" | `availableCountFor(type) -> long` |
196	| "concurrency seam (no-op in core)" | `withSpotGuard(spot, action) -> T` (protected) |
197	
198	```
199	class ParkingLotService:
200	    - floors: List<ParkingFloor>
201	    - strategy: SpotAllocationStrategy
202	    - clock: Supplier<Instant>
203	    - ratePerHourCents: long
204	    - openTickets: Map<String, Ticket>
205	    - ticketCounter: AtomicLong
206	
207	    + ParkingLotService(floors, strategy, clock, ratePerHourCents)
208	    + park(Vehicle) -> Ticket | throws NoSpotAvailableException
209	    + unpark(String ticketId) -> long | throws TicketNotFoundException
210	    + occupiedCount() -> int
211	    + availableCountFor(VehicleType) -> long
212	    # withSpotGuard(spot, action) -> T   (protected hook, no-op here)
213	    - nextTicketId() -> String
214	    - findOpenTicketById(String) -> Ticket
215	    - findSpotById(String) -> ParkingSpot
216	    - computeFee(Instant, Instant) -> long
217	```
218	
219	**(d) Design choices worth calling out**
220	- **Why key `openTickets` by spot id, not ticket id?** Unpark needs to free the *spot*, and a spot can be re-keyed by the next park. Keying by spot id makes "free this spot" O(1) and naturally caps the map at lot capacity.
221	- **Why inject the clock as a `Supplier<Instant>`?** So tests fast-forward time without sleeping. A 90-minute stay is two array writes, not a 90-minute test.
222	- **Why is `withSpotGuard` a no-op here?** This file stays 100% lock-free and readable. The thread-safe subclass overrides this one method — the concurrency diff is one method, not a rewrite.
223	
224	#### (2) `ParkingSpot` — the state machine + lock holder
225	
226	**(a) Role** — One physical spot. The only class allowed to change its own occupancy, and the carrier of the per-spot lock that prevents double-allocation.
227	
228	**(b) State derivation**
229	
230	| Requirement | What this class must remember |
231	|---|---|
232	| "spots of different sizes" | its `size` |
233	| "nearest floor" | its `floorNumber` |
234	| "mark occupied / free" | its `status` and the `vehicle` in it |
235	| "concurrent park safe per spot" | its own `lock` |
236	
237	```
238	class ParkingSpot:
239	    - id: String
240	    - floorNumber: int
241	    - size: SpotSize
242	    - status: SpotStatus
243	    - vehicle: @Nullable Vehicle
244	    - lock: ReentrantLock
245	```
246	
247	**(c) Operations derivation**
248	
249	| Need from requirements | Method on this class |
250	|---|---|
251	| "mark a free spot occupied (guarded)" | `occupy(Vehicle) -> void | throws InvalidSpotStateException` |
252	| "free a spot (idempotent)" | `release() -> void` |
253	| "strategy needs to read availability" | `isFree() -> boolean`, `size()`, `floorNumber()`, `id()` |
254	| "thread-safe subclass needs the lock" | `lock() -> ReentrantLock` |
255	
256	```
257	class ParkingSpot:
258	    - id: String
259	    - floorNumber: int
260	    - size: SpotSize
261	    - status: SpotStatus
262	    - vehicle: @Nullable Vehicle
263	    - lock: ReentrantLock
264	
265	    + ParkingSpot(id, floorNumber, size)
266	    + occupy(Vehicle) -> void | throws InvalidSpotStateException
267	    + release() -> void          # idempotent
268	    + isFree() -> boolean
269	    + status() -> SpotStatus
270	    + vehicle() -> Vehicle | null
271	    + lock() -> ReentrantLock
272	    + id(), floorNumber(), size() -> getters
273	```
274	
275	**(d) Design choices worth calling out**
276	- **Why does the spot own its lock instead of the service holding a `Map<spotId, Lock>`?** Co-locating the lock with the thing it guards means there's no separate map to keep in sync and no "lock the map before the lock" dance. The lock's lifetime is exactly the spot's lifetime.
277	- **Why is `occupy` guarded but `release` idempotent?** Occupying an occupied spot is a real bug (two cars on one spot) — it must throw. Releasing a free spot is a harmless double-free (a real unpark racing a future cleanup sweep) — it must be a silent no-op.
278	
279	#### (3) `SpotAllocationStrategy` — the seam (interface)
280	
281	**(a) Role** — The pluggable policy for "which free spot to use". The orchestrator depends on this abstraction, never on a concrete policy.
282	
283	**(b) State** — none (stateless policy).
284	
285	**(c) Operations**
286	
287	| Need | Method |
288	|---|---|
289	| "find the best free compatible spot" | `findSpot(floors, type) -> Optional<ParkingSpot>` |
290	
291	```
292	interface SpotAllocationStrategy:
293	    + findSpot(List<ParkingFloor> floors, VehicleType type) -> Optional<ParkingSpot>
294	```
295	
296	**(d) Design choices**
297	- **Why `Optional`, not null?** "No spot" is a real answer the caller must unwrap; the service turns an empty Optional into a typed `NoSpotAvailableException`. A null could silently slip through.
298	- **Why return a candidate, not occupy it?** Selection is a pure read; occupying is a guarded write the service does under the lock. Keeping them separate is what lets the *re-check inside the lock* fix the race.
299	
300	#### (4) `BestFitNearestStrategy` — the default policy
301	
302	**(a) Role** — The concrete policy: smallest fitting spot, nearest floor, deterministic tie-break.
303	
304	**(b) State** — one static `Comparator`.
305	
306	**(c) Operations** — implements `findSpot`.
307	
308	```
309	class BestFitNearestStrategy implements SpotAllocationStrategy:
310	    - BEST_FIRST: Comparator<ParkingSpot>   # size rank, then floor, then id
311	
312	    + findSpot(List<ParkingFloor> floors, VehicleType type) -> Optional<ParkingSpot>
313	    - isCompatible(SpotSize, VehicleType) -> boolean
314	```
315	
316	**(d) Design choices**
317	- **Why sort by `(size, floor, id)` and not just size?** Floor gives "nearest"; id gives a *deterministic* pick so the same lot state always returns the same spot — reproducible tests, no flakiness.
318	
319	#### (5) `Vehicle` and `Ticket` — immutable records
320	
321	**(a) Role** — Plain value objects: `Vehicle` is the thing parking; `Ticket` is the entry receipt.
322	
323	**(b)/(c)** — Records; validated at construction.
324	
325	```
326	record Vehicle(String licensePlate, VehicleType type)      # non-blank plate
327	record Ticket(String id, String spotId, Vehicle vehicle, Instant entryTime)  # all non-null
328	```
329	
330	**(d) Design choices**
331	- **Why records?** Immutability for free, value equality, no setters — a ticket once issued can never be mutated.
332	- **Why no `fee` on the ticket?** It's unknown at entry; computed at exit from `entryTime` and the exit time.
333	
334	#### (6) `ThreadSafeParkingLotService` — the concurrency subclass
335	
336	**(a) Role** — The thread-safe version. Overrides exactly one method.
337	
338	**(b) State** — none added.
339	
340	**(c) Operations** — overrides `withSpotGuard`.
341	
342	```

344	    + ThreadSafeParkingLotService(floors, strategy, clock, ratePerHourCents)
345	    # withSpotGuard(spot, action) -> T   (override: lock spot, run, unlock)
346	```
347	
348	**(d) Design choices**
349	- **Why a subclass, not a flag?** The clean core has zero lock code; the diff between clean and safe is one overridden method. The interviewer sees the seam instantly.

424	## 5. Design Patterns & Principles
425	
426	### 5.1 Information Expert (the foundational principle)
427	
428	> 💬 "I followed Information Expert: each class owns the state and behavior closest to the data it has. `ParkingLotService` owns allocation coordination and the open-ticket registry because it's the only thing that sees all floors and all tickets. `ParkingSpot` owns its occupancy and its lock because physical presence — 'is there a car in me right now' — is intrinsic to the spot, not relational to the system. `VehicleType` owns the `fits` rule because compatibility is intrinsic to the vehicle's kind. So nobody reaches across boundaries to mutate state they don't own."
429	
430	### 5.2 Applied patterns
431	
432	**Strategy** — `SpotAllocationStrategy` / `BestFitNearestStrategy`
433	- **Why it fits here:** the rule for picking a spot is *exactly* the thing the business will want to tweak — best-fit today, nearest-door or cheapest-category tomorrow. So I extract the policy and the orchestrator never hardcodes it.
434	- **Interface + impl:** `SpotAllocationStrategy.findSpot(...) → BestFitNearestStrategy`
435	- 🎙️ "The lot doesn't decide which spot — it asks a strategy. Swapping the rule is one new class."
436	- 💬 **Layman:** "It's like the lot doesn't choose the parking space itself — it asks a rule-of-the-day. We can change the rule without rewiring the lot."
437	
438	**Template Method** — `ParkingLotService.withSpotGuard`
439	- **Why it fits here:** I want the clean core to have *zero* lock code, and the thread-safe version to differ by as little as possible. A no-op hook in the base, overridden by the subclass, makes the concurrency diff exactly one method.
440	- **Hook + override:** base `withSpotGuard(spot, action) { return action.get(); }` → subclass wraps it in `spot.lock()`.
441	- 🎙️ "My core file imports no locks. Thread safety is one overridden method."
442	- 💬 **Layman:** "I leave a blank slot where a lock would go. The clean version leaves it blank; the safe version fills it in."
443	
444	### 5.3 SOLID principles
445	
446	- **Single Responsibility:** `ParkingSpot` owns physical state, `SpotAllocationStrategy` owns selection, `ParkingLotService` owns coordination, `computeFee` owns billing. Four reasons to change, four homes — never bundled.
447	- **Open/Closed:** a new allocation policy = a new `SpotAllocationStrategy` class, zero edits to the service. A new pricing rule = a new private helper or injected pricer.
448	- **Dependency Inversion:** `ParkingLotService` depends on the `SpotAllocationStrategy` abstraction and a `Supplier<Instant>` clock abstraction — never on a concrete policy or on `Instant.now()` directly.
449	- **Liskov:** `ThreadSafeParkingLotService` is a drop-in for `ParkingLotService` — it strengthens guarantees (thread safety) without changing the contract.
450	
451	### 5.4 Earn your patterns
452	
453	- **Strategy** — real interface (`SpotAllocationStrategy`) with a real impl (`BestFitNearestStrategy`) the service holds via constructor injection. Survives Cmd-F.
454	- **Template Method** — real `protected withSpotGuard` no-op in §6, real `@Override` in the subclass. Survives Cmd-F.
455	- I did **NOT** claim Singleton (the lot is just constructed once by `Main`, not enforced as single) or Factory (no enum→object translation worth naming). Calling those out would be name-dropping.

459	## 5.5 Core Algorithms & Approach
460	
461	### A. The Key Insight
462	
463	> **Insight:** Make each `ParkingSpot` a self-guarding state machine that *carries its own lock*, and make spot selection a swappable Strategy. To park, lock the one spot you're taking, re-check it's free, occupy it.
464	
465	> **Why it works:** "Which spot is best" is a pure policy with no shared state — it factors cleanly into a Strategy. And "is this spot taken" is intrinsic to the spot, so the spot can guard its own FREE→OCCUPIED transition. Because the lock lives *on the spot*, two cars taking two different spots take two different locks and never block each other. The only contention is two cars fighting for the *same* spot, and the lock plus re-check makes one win cleanly and the other go find another spot.
466	
467	> **What breaks without it:** The naive version inlines `spot.status = OCCUPIED` in the service after a bare `findSpot`. Under load, two threads both see spot L1-1 free (check-then-act), both write OCCUPIED, and two cars are assigned one spot — one ticket silently overwrites the other in the registry. By making the spot guard its own transition *and* requiring its own lock for the write, that bug is impossible by construction.
468	
469	### B. Per-Operation Algorithm
470	
471	#### `park(vehicle)`
472	
473	**(1) Pseudocode**
474	```
475	1. if vehicle is null -> throw ParkingException
476	2. candidate = strategy.findSpot(floors, vehicle.type)
477	3. if candidate is empty -> throw NoSpotAvailableException
478	4. withSpotGuard(candidate):          # no-op in core; per-spot lock in subclass
479	5.     if candidate is NOT free:       # re-check inside the guard
480	6.         return park(vehicle)        # spot was taken meanwhile; retry once
481	7.     candidate.occupy(vehicle)       # FREE -> OCCUPIED, guarded by the spot
482	8.     ticket = new Ticket(nextId, candidate.id, vehicle, clock.now)
483	9.     openTickets[candidate.id] = ticket
484	10.    return ticket
485	```
486	
487	> 🎙️ **Walkthrough (race-prone method):** "This method has the check-then-act race — `findSpot` checks, `occupy` acts, and steps 5-9 are the critical section. Step 2 is where the Strategy fires — the service doesn't know it's best-fit. Step 5 is the non-obvious one: I re-check *inside* the guard, because the strategy's answer is a hint that may be stale by the time I hold the lock. If the spot got taken, I retry the whole park, which re-runs the strategy and finds the next-best spot. In §9 the guard becomes a real per-spot lock, and steps 5-9 become atomic."
488	
489	**(3) Dry run — `park(Vehicle("CAR-1", CAR))`** on a fresh 2-floor lot:
490	
491	| Step | Action | State change |
492	|---|---|---|
493	| 2 | `strategy.findSpot(floors, CAR)` | returns L1-1 (smallest fitting=LARGE, lowest floor, lowest id) |
494	| 4 | enter guard on L1-1 | (no-op in core / lock held in subclass) |
495	| 5 | re-check L1-1 free | free ✓ |
496	| 7 | `L1-1.occupy(CAR-1)` | L1-1.status: FREE → OCCUPIED, vehicle=CAR-1 |
497	| 8 | build ticket | `tkt-1`, spotId=L1-1, entry=08:00 |
498	| 9 | index it | `openTickets={ "L1-1": tkt-1 }` |
499	| 10 | return | caller gets `tkt-1` |
500	
501	> *Edge probe: what if a second car calls `park` while step 7 is mid-flight?* Without a lock both see L1-1 free at step 5 → both occupy → double-allocation. §9 fixes it: the per-spot lock serializes step 5-9, so the loser's re-check at step 5 sees OCCUPIED and recurses to L1-2.
502	
503	#### `unpark(ticketId)`
504	
505	**(1) Pseudocode**
506	```
507	1. if ticketId is null -> throw TicketNotFoundException
508	2. ticket = findOpenTicketById(ticketId)
509	3. if ticket is null -> throw TicketNotFoundException
510	4. spot = findSpotById(ticket.spotId)
511	5. withSpotGuard(spot):
512	6.     spot.release()                  # OCCUPIED -> FREE (idempotent)
513	7.     openTickets.remove(spot.id)     # drop registry entry
514	8. return computeFee(ticket.entryTime, clock.now)
515	```
516	
517	> 🎙️ **Walkthrough (completion path):** "Unpark is two guards then a release-and-bill. Step 2-3 reject unknown tickets. Step 6 is idempotent on purpose — if a future cleanup sweep already freed the spot, `release` is a no-op, no spurious error. Step 8 is the only place fee is computed, and it reads the injected clock, so a test can pretend hours passed."
518	
519	#### `availableCountFor(type)` / `occupiedCount()`
520	
521	**(1) Pseudocode**
522	```
523	occupiedCount:        return openTickets.size                       # O(1)
524	availableCountFor(t): count spots where free AND t.fits(spot.size) # O(total spots)
525	```
526	
527	> 🎙️ **Lookup method:** "`occupiedCount` is an O(1) hit on the registry size — the registry *is* the set of parked cars. `availableCountFor` scans the spots, which is fine at a few hundred spots."
528	
529	### C. Data Structure Choices
530	
531	| Data Structure | Used For | Why THIS (vs alternative) | Complexity |
532	|---|---|---|---|
533	| `Map<String,Ticket>` keyed by **spot id** | open-ticket registry | unpark must free a *spot*; keying by spot id makes "free this spot + drop it" O(1). Keying by ticket id would force a scan of all spots to find which spot to free | O(1) put/remove; O(open) find-by-ticket-id |
534	| `ReentrantLock` per `ParkingSpot` | per-spot critical section | a single global lock serializes the whole building; per-spot locks let N cars park into N different spots fully in parallel. Co-located with the spot so there's no separate lock-map to sync | O(1) acquire |
535	| linear scan + `Comparator.min` in strategy | best-fit selection | n ≤ a few hundred spots per lot; a per-size heap adds sync complexity for zero measurable win at this scale | O(n), n small |
536	| `AtomicLong` counter | ticket ids | readable ids (`tkt-1`) and safe under many threads; a plain `long++` would race | O(1) |
537	| `ConcurrentHashMap` | registry backing | lets the thread-safe subclass read/write the registry from many threads without a separate map lock | O(1) |
538	
539	I chose the **scan** over a per-size heap deliberately. I'd flip to a per-size free-list/heap when a single lot crosses ~a few thousand spots — at that point the scan becomes a measurable hot spot.
540	
541	### D. Tricky Logic
542	
543	> **🧠 Claim:** `park` must re-check the spot is free *inside* the guard, not trust the strategy's answer.
544	> **🔬 Proof:** Thread A's `findSpot` returns L1-1. Before A occupies it, thread B (which also got L1-1) occupies it. If A blindly occupies, two cars share L1-1.
545	> **🛠️ Fix in code:** step 5 in `park` — `if (!candidate.isFree()) return park(vehicle);` — re-checks under the guard and retries, finding the next-best spot.
546	
547	> **🧠 Claim:** `ParkingSpot.release()` must be idempotent.
548	> **🔬 Proof:** A real unpark and a future stale-ticket sweep can both decide a spot is done; whoever runs second would hit "illegal FREE→FREE" and wrongly error on the legitimate path.
549	> **🛠️ Fix in code:** `release()` only flips when `status == OCCUPIED`; otherwise it returns silently.
550	
551	> **🧠 Claim:** A regular car must never take a handicapped spot, and vice-versa.
552	> **🔬 Proof:** Pure rank comparison (`spot.rank >= vehicle.minRank`) would let a CAR grab a HANDICAPPED spot if HANDICAPPED had a high rank, or block handicapped vehicles if it had a low rank.
553	> **🛠️ Fix in code:** `VehicleType.fits` special-cases HANDICAPPED with exact two-way equality before falling back to the rank test.
554	
555	> **🧠 Claim:** Fee must round partial hours up with a one-hour minimum.
556	> **🔬 Proof:** Integer division `minutes/60` would bill 0 for a 30-minute stay — free parking by accident.
557	> **🛠️ Fix in code:** `(minutes + 59) / 60`, then `if (hours == 0) hours = 1`.
558	
559	> **🧠 Claim:** Billing in `long` cents, never `double`.
560	> **🔬 Proof:** `0.1 + 0.2 != 0.3` in floating point — money drifts.
561	> **🛠️ Fix in code:** `ratePerHourCents` is a `long`; the fee is `hours * ratePerHourCents`.
562	
563	### E. The "what I'd write WRONG first" anti-example
564	
565	> **❌ Naive version a junior would write:**
566	> ```
567	> Ticket park(Vehicle v):
568	>     spot = findAnyFreeSpot(v.type)     # bare lookup
569	>     spot.status = OCCUPIED             # direct write, no guard, no lock
570	>     return new Ticket(...)
571	> ```
572	> **Why it's wrong:** two failure modes. (1) The direct status write bypasses the spot's state machine — illegal transitions become possible. (2) The find-then-write is check-then-act — under concurrency two callers both see the same spot free and both occupy it. The correct version delegates selection to a Strategy, calls `spot.occupy()` so the entity guards itself, and lifts the check-and-occupy under a per-spot lock with a re-check (§9).
573

706	### `ParkingSpot` — the state machine + lock holder
707	
708	The only class allowed to change a spot's status, and the carrier of the per-spot lock.
709	
710	```java
711	package com.interview.lld.parking.model;
712	
713	import java.util.concurrent.locks.ReentrantLock;
714	import javax.annotation.Nullable;
715	
716	public class ParkingSpot {
717	    private final String id;
718	    private final int floorNumber;
719	    private final SpotSize size;
720	    private SpotStatus status;
721	    @Nullable private Vehicle vehicle;
722	    private final ReentrantLock lock = new ReentrantLock();   // per-spot lock
723	
724	    public ParkingSpot(String id, int floorNumber, SpotSize size) {
725	        this.id = id; this.floorNumber = floorNumber; this.size = size;
726	        this.status = SpotStatus.FREE; this.vehicle = null;
727	    }
728	
729	    public String id() { return id; }
730	    public int floorNumber() { return floorNumber; }
731	    public SpotSize size() { return size; }
732	    public SpotStatus status() { return status; }
733	    public boolean isFree() { return status == SpotStatus.FREE; }
734	    @Nullable public Vehicle vehicle() { return vehicle; }
735	    public ReentrantLock lock() { return lock; }
736	
737	    public void occupy(Vehicle v) {
738	        // WHY: guard the transition — a non-free spot cannot be occupied again.
739	        if (status != SpotStatus.FREE) {
740	            throw new InvalidSpotStateException("spot " + id + " is not FREE, cannot occupy");
741	        }
742	        this.status = SpotStatus.OCCUPIED;
743	        this.vehicle = v;
744	    }
745	
746	    public void release() {
747	        // WHY: idempotent — releasing a free spot is a safe no-op.
748	        if (status == SpotStatus.OCCUPIED) {
749	            this.status = SpotStatus.FREE;
750	            this.vehicle = null;
751	        }
752	    }
753	}
754	```

756	### `SpotAllocationStrategy` + `BestFitNearestStrategy`
757	
758	The seam, then the default policy.
759	
760	```java
761	package com.interview.lld.parking.strategy;
762	
763	import java.util.List;
764	import java.util.Optional;
765	import com.interview.lld.parking.model.ParkingFloor;
766	import com.interview.lld.parking.model.ParkingSpot;
767	import com.interview.lld.parking.model.VehicleType;
768	
769	public interface SpotAllocationStrategy {
770	    Optional<ParkingSpot> findSpot(List<ParkingFloor> floors, VehicleType vehicleType);
771	}
772	```
773	
774	```java
775	package com.interview.lld.parking.strategy;
776	
777	import java.util.Comparator;
778	import java.util.List;
779	import java.util.Optional;
780	import com.interview.lld.parking.model.*;
781	
782	public class BestFitNearestStrategy implements SpotAllocationStrategy {
783	    // smallest fitting size, then nearest floor, then id (deterministic)
784	    private static final Comparator<ParkingSpot> BEST_FIRST =
785	        Comparator.comparingInt((ParkingSpot s) -> s.size().rank())
786	            .thenComparingInt(ParkingSpot::floorNumber)
787	            .thenComparing(ParkingSpot::id);
788	
789	    @Override
790	    public Optional<ParkingSpot> findSpot(List<ParkingFloor> floors, VehicleType type) {
791	        return floors.stream()
792	            .flatMap(f -> f.spots().stream())
793	            .filter(ParkingSpot::isFree)
794	            .filter(s -> type.fits(s.size()))
795	            .min(BEST_FIRST);
796	    }
797	}
798	```
799	
800	#### Approach (the one comparison — how `findSpot` locates the right spot)
801	
802	**Approach 1 — first-free-that-fits (rejected):**
803	```
804	for each spot: if free and fits -> return it
805	```
806	> **Challenges:** returns *any* fitting spot, so a motorcycle can grab a LARGE spot while a COMPACT sits empty — then a car arrives and there's no LARGE left. It wastes the scarce big spots. No notion of "best".
807	
808	**Approach 2 — per-size heap of free spots (rejected for this scale):**
809	```
810	Map<SpotSize, PriorityQueue<ParkingSpot>> freeBySize
811	park: pop from the smallest fitting size's heap
812	unpark: push the freed spot back into its size's heap
813	```
814	> **Challenges:** state now lives in two places — the spot's status AND the heaps — and they must stay in sync. Forget to push on unpark and the spot vanishes from availability forever. I'd flip to this only when a lot crosses ~a few thousand spots.
815	
816	**Chosen approach — scan + min by `(size, floor, id)`:**
817	```
818	stream all spots -> filter free & compatible -> min by best-first comparator
819	```
820	> **Why it wins:** single source of truth — the spot owns its own status, no heaps to sync. Best-fit falls out of the comparator's first key, nearest-floor from the second, determinism from the third. O(n) but n is a few hundred — microseconds.

855	#### `park(Vehicle)` — the pipeline
856	
857	**1. Role:** the core deposit workflow — find the best spot and tie it to a ticket in one guarded step.
858	
859	**2. Core logic (happy path):**
860	1. Ask the strategy for the best free compatible spot
861	2. Enter the per-spot guard
862	3. Re-check the spot is free (it may have been taken since step 1)
863	4. Occupy it (the spot guards the transition)
864	5. Build a ticket, index it by spot id, return it
865	
866	**3. Edge cases:**
867	- Null vehicle
868	- No compatible spot anywhere (lot full)
869	- Spot taken between selection and occupy (the race)
870	
871	**4. Code:**
872	```java
873	public Ticket park(Vehicle vehicle) {
874	    if (vehicle == null) {
875	        throw new ParkingException("vehicle must not be null");
876	    }
877	    ParkingSpot candidate = strategy.findSpot(floors, vehicle.type())
878	        .orElseThrow(() -> new NoSpotAvailableException(
879	            "no free spot for vehicle type " + vehicle.type()));
880	
881	    return withSpotGuard(candidate, () -> {
882	        if (!candidate.isFree()) {        // WHY: re-check inside the guard
883	            return park(vehicle);          //      spot taken meanwhile -> retry
884	        }
885	        candidate.occupy(vehicle);         // FREE -> OCCUPIED, guarded by the spot
886	        Ticket ticket = new Ticket(nextTicketId(), candidate.id(), vehicle, clock.get());
887	        openTickets.put(candidate.id(), ticket);
888	        return ticket;
889	    });
890	}
891	```
892	
893	**5. Reasoning:** `park` is the pipeline. The interesting line is the re-check `if (!candidate.isFree()) return park(vehicle);` — the strategy's answer is only a *hint* that can go stale between selection and the moment we hold the guard, so I look again under the lock and, if the spot's gone, I re-run the whole thing, which finds the next-best spot. Notice I'm *not* validating the vehicle type inside `park` — that's the strategy's job, which simply returns no spot for an incompatible vehicle. In the clean core `withSpotGuard` is a no-op, so this reads as straight-line code; the only thing that changes for thread safety is that the guard becomes a real per-spot lock. The `occupy` call goes through the spot's own state machine rather than a direct status write, so even if the lock were somehow removed, an illegal double-occupy throws instead of silently overwriting a car.
894	
895	> 🎙️ Say: "Strategy picks a spot, then inside the guard I re-check it's free and occupy it. The re-check is what beats the race — the spot might've been taken since I looked."
896	> ⚠️ "What if two cars get the same spot from the strategy?" → "Inside the guard I re-check `isFree`. The loser sees OCCUPIED and recurses to the next-best spot. With the per-spot lock in §9, only one car is ever inside the guard for that spot at a time."
897	
898	#### `unpark(String)` — the completion path
899	
900	**1. Role:** free the spot and bill by time.
901	
902	**2. Core logic:** look up the ticket → find its spot → under the guard, release and drop the ticket → compute the fee.
903	
904	**3. Edge cases:** null ticket id, unknown/used ticket id, ticket pointing at an unknown spot (corrupt config).
905	
906	**4. Code:**
907	```java
908	public long unpark(String ticketId) {
909	    if (ticketId == null) {
910	        throw new TicketNotFoundException("ticketId must not be null");
911	    }
912	    Ticket ticket = findOpenTicketById(ticketId);
913	    if (ticket == null) {
914	        throw new TicketNotFoundException("no open ticket with id " + ticketId);
915	    }
916	    ParkingSpot spot = findSpotById(ticket.spotId());
917	    withSpotGuard(spot, () -> {
918	        spot.release();                 // OCCUPIED -> FREE (idempotent)
919	        openTickets.remove(spot.id());  // so the same ticket can't unpark twice
920	        return null;
921	    });
922	    return computeFee(ticket.entryTime(), clock.get());
923	}
924	```
925	
926	**5. Reasoning:** `unpark` is two guards then a release-and-bill. The `spot.release()` is idempotent, so a real unpark racing a future cleanup sweep is safe — whoever runs first frees the spot, the second call does nothing. I `remove` from the registry inside the same guard so the same ticket id can never be unparked twice. The fee is computed *outside* the guard from the injected clock — releasing the spot has nothing to do with reading the entry time, so I don't hold the lock during billing.
927	
928	> 🎙️ Say: "Look up the ticket, free the spot, drop it from the registry, then bill. Release is idempotent so a double-free is harmless."
929	> ⚠️ "What if someone unparks the same ticket twice?" → "The first unpark removes it from the registry, so the second `findOpenTicketById` returns null and we throw `TicketNotFoundException`."
930

932	
933	```java
934	private String nextTicketId() {
935	    return "tkt-" + ticketCounter.incrementAndGet();   // readable, thread-safe ids
936	}
937	
938	private Ticket findOpenTicketById(String ticketId) {
939	    for (Ticket t : openTickets.values()) {            // small set: only parked cars
940	        if (t.id().equals(ticketId)) return t;
941	    }
942	    return null;
943	}
944	
945	private ParkingSpot findSpotById(String spotId) {
946	    for (ParkingFloor floor : floors) {
947	        for (ParkingSpot spot : floor.spots()) {
948	            if (spot.id().equals(spotId)) return spot;
949	        }
950	    }
951	    throw new TicketNotFoundException("ticket points at unknown spot " + spotId);
952	}
953	
954	private long computeFee(Instant entry, Instant exit) {
955	    long minutes = Math.max(0, Duration.between(entry, exit).toMinutes());
956	    long hours = (minutes + 59) / 60;     // round up partial hours
957	    if (hours == 0) hours = 1;            // one-hour minimum
958	    return hours * ratePerHourCents;      // cents, no float
959	}
960	```
961	
962	`computeFee` rounds partial hours up with a one-hour minimum, all in `long` cents. The clock is injected so a test fast-forwards time instead of sleeping.

1047	## 8. Key Flow (sequence diagram)
1048	
1049	**Which flow and why:** I'm showing `park` because it touches every class — the service, the strategy, and the spot's state machine — and it's where the Strategy fires and the race lives.
1050	
1051	```mermaid
1052	sequenceDiagram
1053	    actor Driver
1054	    participant Svc as ParkingLotService
1055	    participant Strat as BestFitNearestStrategy
1056	    participant Spot as ParkingSpot
1057	
1058	    Driver->>Svc: park(vehicle)
1059	    Svc->>Strat: findSpot(floors, type)
1060	    Strat->>Strat: scan free compatible spots then pick best by size floor id
1061	    Strat-->>Svc: Optional of candidate spot
1062	    Svc->>Spot: withSpotGuard then re-check isFree
1063	    Spot-->>Svc: free is true
1064	    Svc->>Spot: occupy(vehicle)
1065	    Spot->>Spot: FREE to OCCUPIED guarded transition
1066	    Svc->>Svc: build ticket then index by spot id
1067	    Svc-->>Driver: Ticket
1068	```
1069	
1070	> 🎙️ **Walk the diagram arrow by arrow:** "The driver calls `park` on the service. The service doesn't pick the spot itself — it delegates to `findSpot` on the strategy, which scans free compatible spots and returns the best one by size, floor, then id. That's the Strategy firing. Now the service enters the guard on that spot and re-checks `isFree` — the interesting moment, because the candidate could be stale. With the spot confirmed free, the service calls `occupy`, and the *spot itself* flips FREE→OCCUPIED through its guarded transition — the service never writes the status directly. Finally the service builds the ticket, indexes it by spot id, and hands it back."
1071	
1072	> **Why the sequence shows the design is good:** the service coordinates but implements no business logic — selection delegates to the strategy and the status change delegates to the spot's state machine. That's Open/Closed and Information Expert at runtime.

1076	## 9. Concurrency, Thread-Safety, Edge Cases & Extensibility
1077	
1078	**DEFAULT: describe verbally. Write code only if asked.**
1079	
1080	🗣️ **Step 1 — Name the exact race:** "The race is in `park`. Two cars call it at the same time. The strategy hands both the same free spot, L1-1. Both pass the `isFree` re-check, both call `occupy`, both succeed — two cars on one spot, and one ticket overwrites the other in the registry. That's a check-then-act race: the check (`isFree`) and the act (`occupy`) aren't one atomic step."
1081	
1082	🗣️ **Step 2 — Solution:** "Each `ParkingSpot` carries its own `ReentrantLock`. Before the check-and-occupy, the thread takes *that spot's* lock. While it holds the lock, no other thread can enter for that spot. After occupy, it releases. Two cars racing for L1-1 now race for L1-1's lock — one wins, the other waits, then its re-check sees OCCUPIED and recurses to the next-best spot."
1083	
1084	🗣️ **Step 3 — Trade-off:** "I chose *per-spot* locks, not one global lock. A global lock is correct but serializes the whole building — one car parking blocks every other car. Per-spot locks let a hundred cars park into a hundred different spots fully in parallel; only two cars fighting for the *same* spot ever serialize, and that's microseconds."
1085	
1086	🗣️ **Step 4 — Distributed:** "For multiple servers, an in-process lock only protects one JVM. I'd push atomicity to the database with a compare-and-set: `UPDATE spot SET status='OCCUPIED' WHERE id=? AND status='FREE'`. Whoever's update affects 0 rows lost the race and retries or gets a clean error."
1087	
1088	**Concurrency vocabulary**
1089	
1090	| Term | Plain meaning | Where it appears here |
1091	|---|---|---|
1092	| Race condition | Two threads touch shared state at once; result depends on timing | `findSpot` + `occupy` in `park` |
1093	| Critical section | The block that must run as one atomic step | the re-check + `occupy` + index block |
1094	| Check-then-act | Check a condition, then act — but state changed in between | "saw FREE, then occupied" |
1095	| Atomic | All-or-nothing; no half-done state observable | what the per-spot lock guarantees |
1096	| Mutex / lock | A token only one thread holds at a time | `ReentrantLock` on each `ParkingSpot` |
1097	| Reentrant | Same thread can re-acquire a lock it holds | a guarded method could call another on the same spot |
1098	| Lock granularity | How much one lock covers — coarse vs fine | we chose per-SPOT, not one global lock |
1099	| Pessimistic locking | Grab the lock before touching state | the in-process `ReentrantLock` |
1100	| Optimistic locking | Don't lock; write conditionally and retry | the DB compare-and-set for distributed |
1101	| Compare-and-set | Atomic "update only if value is still X" | `WHERE status='FREE'` |
1102	| Deadlock | Two threads each hold a lock the other needs | avoided: we hold only ONE spot lock at a time |
1103	| Starvation | A thread never gets the lock | not a risk; locks are short-held |
1104	
1105	**Concurrency follow-up Q&A**
1106	
1107	> ❓ "Why per-spot locks and not one global lock?"
1108	> "A global lock serializes every park in the building through one bottleneck even though different spots share no state. Per-spot locks let cars going to different spots run fully parallel; only same-spot contenders serialize, microseconds each."
1109	
1110	> ❓ "Why `ReentrantLock` and not a plain non-reentrant lock?"
1111	> "If a guarded method ever calls another guarded method on the same spot, a non-reentrant lock would deadlock on the second acquire by the same thread. Reentrant lets the owning thread re-enter and only fully releases at the outermost unlock. Today my call graph holds one spot lock at a time, but reentrant is the safe default and costs nothing."
1112	
1113	> ❓ "What's the deadlock risk?"
1114	> "Deadlock needs two locks acquired in different orders. Here a `park` holds exactly ONE spot lock and never grabs a second while holding it — the strategy scan happens *before* the lock, and billing happens *after*. No lock-ordering cycle, no deadlock."
1115	
1116	> ❓ "Why is the strategy scan outside the lock?"
1117	> "The scan only reads spot status; it's a hint. Holding a lock across a scan of every spot would serialize the whole lot. I scan lock-free, then take just the one candidate spot's lock and re-check under it — so the expensive part is parallel and only the tiny check-and-occupy is serialized."
1118	
1119	> ❓ "Pessimistic or optimistic — which and why?"
1120	> "Pessimistic in-process: the critical section is tiny (a re-check and a status flip) and same-spot contention is rare, so a short-held lock is simpler than a retry loop. For the distributed case I flip to optimistic — a DB compare-and-set — because you can't hold a JVM lock across servers."
1121	
1122	> ❓ "What if the lock-holder thread crashes mid-critical-section?"
1123	> "In-process, a crash takes the JVM down, so the half-done state dies with it — no orphaned lock. In the DB version the compare-and-set is one atomic statement: it either fully committed or didn't."
1124	
1125	❓ **IF ASKED — the thread-safe version:**
1126	
1127	**Part A — the no-op hook + subclass:**
1128	```java
1129	// ─── §6 base (clean): the hook does nothing ──────────────────────────────────
1130	protected <T> T withSpotGuard(ParkingSpot spot, Supplier<T> action) {
1131	    return action.get();
1132	}
1133	
1134	// ─── §9 subclass (thread-safe): override ONLY this one method ─────────────────
1135	@Override
1136	protected <T> T withSpotGuard(ParkingSpot spot, Supplier<T> action) {
1137	    ReentrantLock lock = spot.lock();      // each spot carries its OWN lock
1138	    lock.lock();
1139	    try {
1140	        return action.get();
1141	    } finally {
1142	        lock.unlock();                     // always unlock, even on exception
1143	    }
1144	}
1145	```
1146	- **Why `ReentrantLock`:** a guarded method may call another guarded helper on the same spot; reentrant avoids a self-deadlock.
1147	- **Why per-spot, not global:** different spots can be parked into in parallel — no global bottleneck.
1148	- **No double-lock-map idiom needed:** the lock lives *on the spot*, so there's no `Map<id,Lock>` to lock first — the spot reference IS the lock owner.
1149	
1150	**Part B — the exact modified method.** `park` itself does NOT change between §6 and §9 — the only difference is which `withSpotGuard` runs. That's the whole point of the Template Method:
1151	```java

1153	public Ticket park(Vehicle vehicle) {
1154	    if (vehicle == null) {
1155	        throw new ParkingException("vehicle must not be null");
1156	    }
1157	    ParkingSpot candidate = strategy.findSpot(floors, vehicle.type())   // outside lock: read-only scan
1158	        .orElseThrow(() -> new NoSpotAvailableException(
1159	            "no free spot for vehicle type " + vehicle.type()));
1160	    return withSpotGuard(candidate, () -> {        // §6: no-op | §9: locks candidate
1161	        if (!candidate.isFree()) {                 // re-check INSIDE the lock
1162	            return park(vehicle);                  // taken meanwhile -> retry next-best
1163	        }
1164	        candidate.occupy(vehicle);                 // now atomic with the re-check
1165	        Ticket ticket = new Ticket(nextTicketId(), candidate.id(), vehicle, clock.get());
1166	        openTickets.put(candidate.id(), ticket);
1167	        return ticket;
1168	    });
1169	}
1170	```
1171	
1172	> 🎙️ "The diff from §6 to §9 is *zero lines in `park`* — only the `withSpotGuard` hook changes from a no-op to a per-spot lock. Outside the guard: the strategy scan, which only reads. Inside the guard: the re-check and occupy, which must be one atomic step. The template-method hook means §6 never changes; I just override `withSpotGuard` in the subclass."
1173	
1174	**Part C — the concurrency test (real, passing):** 50 threads, 5 spots, a `CyclicBarrier` so all 50 hit `park` at the same instant:
